All right, you're back. So here we go. Time to get specific now in how you actually go about creating a customer for life. And it starts by turning your new customers into raving fans of the following three things. And they should not surprise you, by the way, they are the three 10s. You want to turn into raving fans of number one, your product, number two, raving fans about you, the salesperson, right? And also raving fans about your company, those three 10s, right? Now, in reality, there are actually two sides to this equation. First, there's the technical side of the equation in terms of how you correctly onboard a new client and successfully guide them through the adoption phase, meaning when they actually start using the product they had earned us throughout the whole company relying on the features and benefits and so forth. That's number one. And number two, there are the nuances here that will focus on in terms of how you use certain language patterns and straight line communication strategies that allow you to accomplish the following three things. Number one, you want to keep those all important three 10s at an elevated level. Without that, you basically can't go anywhere fast, right? That's a must. Number two, you then want to lay the foundation for those massively lucrative upsell opportunities and cross-sells. It's number two. And then number three, you want to generate massive referrals along the way. So, let's first go through this make or break process of onboarding, which if you don't execute correctly, you're basically screwed. You get it? And by the way, I've been through this both as a salesperson and as a business owner. Doesn't matter. If you don't onboard correctly either on the company, so I'm meeting as a customer success division that does it, or the salesperson themselves does it, you got a real problem here. It's not going to end well. So, if you have a situation like that and every company is different, so I can't say exactly what's going on with your company, but there are certain aspects to what you're offering if they're a bit complicated or cumbersome or can lead to overwhelm when you do the slightest way, then you better make sure that somebody is directly reaching out to this customer or at least sending them some material to make sure they know exactly how to get started and they feel good about it. A perfect example in a weird way is Apple. In other words, when you buy something from Apple, there's no one telling you what to do or onboarding you, but what they have is a product that is beautifully packaged and intuitive from the moment that you want to wrap it. In other words, no one's to tell you, use your finger, press this button, swipe this way. You kind of know because the way they've set this up is just basically perfectly intuitive. That's why everyone loves Apple products so much, and they also work awesome, right? So, even if no one's there to tell you what to do, if your company progress like that, that's okay, but it has to be a design that allows people to easily adapt to it. Okay? So, just so you know here, you can't just say, well, it's my company's problem if you're a salesperson. Yes, you want to hope that your company's handling this in the best way possible, but companies that are growing fast sometimes get overwhelmed. They don't have the manpower, they don't have the bandwidth to correctly onboard every single customer the way they should be onboarded. In that case, you as a great sales person, you want to pick up that slack yourself and make sure that customer gets onboarded correctly and feels good about their purchase. And we'll get to that later exactly how you do that. You want to make sure there's no complications, no confusion, because if there is, well, either they will cancel or they'll sign up and bar from you again. You get it? Okay, great. So, let's get specific here in terms of things that you can actually do to correctly onboard. Firstly, here's the cardinal sin for salespeople. If you do not want to over promise and under deliver or put another way, you always want to give more than you pitch. In the end, this is all about the management of expectation. For example, if you promise, promise, promise, promise, promise, promise, and you still deliver significant value, but not quite as much value as you promise, then guess what? You set yourself up to have a very angry customer on your hands, which is really sad, because you just deliver massive value anyway. Yet because you made unrealistic promises that to find expectation, you're still going to have a very disappointed customer on your hands, which then sabotages your ability to create a custom flight meeting, get upsells, cross-tells, and referrals. You get it? So, how do you avoid doing this? Well, the simple answer is it starts with a firm policy from you personally to always make sure that you are under promising and over delivering, and there are five ways you can ensure that. Number one, ready for this? Using a script. This is the easiest way to protect yourself from getting swept away or carried away in the moment, and that's what typically happens. No bad intentions. You start talking, you're excited, you make a promise, can't keep it, and now you've set yourself up for a problem. The key here is to remember at the end of the day, as you're constructing your presentation, it's all about the truth well told. In other words, you want to be as sexy as possible with your pitch compelling as possible to get your point across in a way that moves your prospect logically and emotionally, right? But without making the sort of unrealistic promises, they would end up biting it in the butt on the other side and blow up the relationship during the onboarding and adoption phase. Makes sense? Okay, great. Now, here's the deal. This is great news. Those two outcomes are not mutually exclusive, meaning you can be sexy with your pitch, be very compelling, and also not exaggerate. You're just going to take the time here to strike that right balance. That's what scripting was all about. Okay, and to that end, let's move on to number two here. One of the most common hot spots in your script is when you're focusing on the energy in benefits outside of the equation as you close. See, this is where most salespeople tend to fall down here. They'll say something to the prospect about how easy it is to get started. There's no effort required, and there actually is. Remember what I said? I said, don't do that. Don't exaggerate the energy in benefits out. Just make sure that you do whatever you can to make it as easy as possible. Here's a perfect story that illustrates this. I was doing the training in England maybe about a year ago, and it was a pension company that had found this loophole in the law that allowed people to roll over their pensions seamlessly. They should have signed some documents, right? And they would get a much higher rate of return. So they had a closing rate that was north of 75 or 80%. But here's the problem. They'd get a yes on the phone. They would send the documents for the person to sign, the person had assigned their name, fell some information, right? And then only 15 or 20% would come back. The rest of them fell out, and they couldn't understand why. And what happened was when I analyzed their script, they were making it sound so simple, so easy. But really, what happened when they got the documents, they actually had to go through a whole bunch of information, find an old form that had that current pension number. So there was more work than they were saying. And as a result of that, they did not manage the expectation correctly. People didn't want to do it. So it was the solution. Very simple. In this case, I had them intervene on the company sign and had them send messengers, they were bonded messengers to actually go to people's homes and physically get them to sign, help them collect the documents because their margins were so high that you could do it there. So that was an example how to solve it on the company side. But just notice where the problem started. It started because when they were closed and like it's so easy to get started, they were following the straight line, but not realizing that if you over exaggerate the simplicity of getting started, meaning you make the energy in so low, but it really is not that low, you got a problem, reality hits you. So it's not going to work out well. All the way to resolve it would have been to change the language pad to explain, yeah, there's very little work to do, but here's what you got to do. And we do everything else for you. In fact, I'll even call you on the phone and guide you through it so you can offset that. But the point is be very careful not to over promise it on deliver on the energy in benefits outside of the equation. You get it? Now, number three, here's another one, your looping patterns. Now in this case, don't throw the proverbial kitchen sink at your prospect when you're looping. If you can't deliver that proverbial kitchen sink, in other words, don't keep promising more and more stuff as you're building certainty. This is the trap people follow. They don't script out their loops. That's why I always say have two, three, four language patterns prepared in advance that allows you to keep talking about your product in a way that's sexy, compelling and accurate. Here's another example, number four, delivery times. Now this is an industry specific issue, but in some really egregious cases I've seen, like for instance, the furniture business or any type of repair like computers, automobiles, auto body, where 90% of the time it's not shoddy workmanship that destroys the relationship. It's actually the failure to deliver the supposed solution on time. And for example, you know the worst violence of this at all? Ready? Home builders. Oh my God. This is a great movie, The Money Pick with Tom Hanks with Running Jokers. How much longer? Two more weeks, three months later, two more weeks. And it's true. Things have a way of getting delayed in that business. Permits are required, the weather interferes, products aren't delivered to you, to the builder on time, right? So just be very careful if you're in any industry where it's not so cotton dry and how quickly you can actually deliver the solution. If it's a service or if it's a product, how quickly you can marshal all the resources required and get it shipped to that person so they have the solution in hand. Don't get in the habit of under delivering it over promising. It will not end well. I promise. Okay. And with that, let's go to step number five now, which are the deployment of your customer onboarding and adoption strategies. And these are absolutely crucial. Before we go there, I want to first stop. I want you to take a quick quiz, go through some exercises, and when you get back as soon as possible, let's roll forward. You're in a great spot. I will see you soon.
